questions.stem: A normed space is called complete (a Banach space) when which condition holds?
options: Every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence.
Every Cauchy sequence converges to a limit in the space.
The norm is induced by an inner product.
questions.explanation: Completeness means every Cauchy sequence converges to a point of the space. This is exactly the Banach space property; convergence of bounded subsequences is compactness, and an inner product gives a Hilbert (hence Banach) space but is not required for completeness alone.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The Riesz-Fischer theorem asserts which property of $L^p$ spaces for $1 \le p \le \infty$?
options: $L^p$ is complete, hence a Banach space.
$L^p$ has an orthonormal basis for every $p$.
questions.explanation: The Riesz-Fischer theorem states that $L^p$ is complete for every $1 \le p \le \infty$, so it is a Banach space. Separability holds for $1 \le p < \infty$ on nice spaces but is a separate fact, and orthonormal bases exist only in the Hilbert case $p = 2$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Among the $L^p$ spaces, which one is a Hilbert space (its norm comes from an inner product)?
questions.explanation: Only $L^2$ carries an inner product $\langle f, g \rangle = \int f\bar g\,d\mu$ whose induced norm is $\|\cdot\|_2$; this norm satisfies the parallelogram law. The other $L^p$ norms violate the parallelogram law, so they are Banach but not Hilbert.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The inner product on $L^2(X, \mu)$ (over the complex field) is given by which expression?
questions.explanation: The $L^2$ inner product is $\langle f, g \rangle = \int_X f\bar g\,d\mu$, which is linear in $f$, conjugate-linear in $g$, and satisfies $\langle f, f \rangle = \|f\|_2^2$. The other expressions are not sesquilinear forms.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: A set $\{e_n\}$ in an inner product space is orthonormal when which conditions hold?
options: $\langle e_n, e_m \rangle = \delta_{nm}$ (orthogonal and each of unit norm).
questions.explanation: Orthonormality means $\langle e_n, e_m \rangle = \delta_{nm}$: distinct vectors are orthogonal and each has unit norm. The Kronecker delta captures both conditions at once.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: For an orthonormal set $\{e_n\}$ and $f \in L^2$, the Fourier coefficient of $f$ with respect to $e_n$ is which quantity?
questions.explanation: The Fourier (generalized) coefficient is $c_n = \langle f, e_n \rangle$, the projection of $f$ onto $e_n$. These coefficients are the components of $f$ in the orthonormal directions and appear in Bessel's inequality and Parseval's identity.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Bessel's inequality states which bound for an orthonormal set $\{e_n\}$ and $f \in L^2$?
questions.explanation: Bessel's inequality reads $\sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2 \le \|f\|^2$ for any orthonormal set. It says the energy captured by the coefficients cannot exceed the total energy of $f$; equality for all $f$ is the stronger completeness (Parseval) condition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: An orthonormal set $\{e_n\}$ in a Hilbert space is complete (an orthonormal basis) precisely when which equality holds for every $f$?
options: Bessel's inequality is strict for every $f$.
Parseval's identity $\sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2 = \|f\|^2$ holds for every $f$.
questions.explanation: Completeness of the orthonormal set is equivalent to Parseval's identity $\sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2 = \|f\|^2$ holding for all $f$, that is Bessel's inequality becomes equality. Equivalently, the only vector orthogonal to all $e_n$ is $0$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which characterization of a complete orthonormal set $\{e_n\}$ is equivalent to Parseval's identity?
options: If $\langle f, e_n \rangle = 0$ for all $n$, then $f = 0$.
questions.explanation: A standard equivalence: an orthonormal set is complete iff no nonzero vector is orthogonal to every $e_n$, i.e. $\langle f, e_n \rangle = 0$ for all $n$ forces $f = 0$. This is precisely the condition under which Parseval's identity and the expansion $f = \sum_n \langle f, e_n \rangle e_n$ hold.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The Riesz-Fischer theorem also guarantees that for a complete orthonormal set $\{e_n\}$, any square-summable sequence $(c_n)$ with $\sum |c_n|^2 < \infty$ is realized how?
options: There is an $f \in L^2$ with $\langle f, e_n \rangle = c_n$, given by $f = \sum_n c_n e_n$.
Such $f$ exists only if the $c_n$ are eventually zero.
questions.explanation: Riesz-Fischer says the coefficient map $L^2 \to \ell^2$ is onto: given $\sum |c_n|^2 < \infty$, the series $\sum_n c_n e_n$ converges in $L^2$ to some $f$ with $\langle f, e_n \rangle = c_n$. Completeness of $L^2$ is exactly what makes this partial-sum sequence converge.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Convergence in $L^2$ norm of $f_n \to f$ means which statement?
questions.explanation: Convergence in $L^2$ is convergence in the norm: $\|f_n - f\|_2 \to 0$, equivalently $\int |f_n - f|^2 \to 0$. This is generally weaker than pointwise or uniform convergence, though a subsequence converges almost everywhere.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Why is completeness of $L^p$ important for the theory of Fourier series in $L^2$?
options: Because it makes every function continuous.
Because it ensures the partial sums of a Fourier series, being Cauchy in $L^2$, actually converge to an element of $L^2$.
Because it forces pointwise convergence everywhere.
questions.explanation: The partial sums $\sum_{n \le N} c_n e_n$ of a square-summable Fourier series form a Cauchy sequence in $L^2$. Completeness (Riesz-Fischer) guarantees they converge to a genuine $L^2$ limit, which is the function with those Fourier coefficients.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: For a complete orthonormal set, the expansion of $f \in L^2$ is which series, convergent in $L^2$?
questions.explanation: The generalized Fourier expansion is $f = \sum_n \langle f, e_n \rangle e_n$, converging in the $L^2$ norm when $\{e_n\}$ is a complete orthonormal set. The coefficients are the projections onto each basis direction.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Parseval's identity in the form involving two functions $f, g$ and a complete orthonormal set reads:
options: $\langle f, g \rangle = \sum_n \langle f, e_n \rangle \overline{\langle g, e_n \rangle}$.
$\langle f, g \rangle = \sum_n \langle f, e_n \rangle \langle g, e_n \rangle$.
questions.explanation: The polarized Parseval identity is $\langle f, g \rangle = \sum_n \langle f, e_n \rangle \overline{\langle g, e_n \rangle}$, reducing to $\|f\|^2 = \sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2$ when $g = f$. The conjugate on the second factor is required for the complex inner product.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which property does $L^1$ have that distinguishes it from $L^2$?
options: $L^1$ is complete but is not a Hilbert space.
questions.explanation: By Riesz-Fischer, $L^1$ is complete (a Banach space), but its norm does not come from an inner product (the parallelogram law fails), so it is not a Hilbert space. Only $L^2$ enjoys the inner-product structure.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: If $\{e_n\}$ is orthonormal and $f = \sum_{k=1}^{N} c_k e_k$ is a finite linear combination, what is $\|f\|^2$?
questions.explanation: By orthonormality the cross terms vanish: $\|f\|^2 = \langle \sum_k c_k e_k, \sum_j c_j e_j \rangle = \sum_k |c_k|^2$. This finite Pythagorean identity is the seed of Parseval's identity in the infinite case.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The best approximation to $f \in L^2$ from the span of $\{e_1, \ldots, e_N\}$ (orthonormal) is the partial sum $S_N = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \langle f, e_n \rangle e_n$. Why is it optimal?
options: Because $f - S_N$ is orthogonal to the span, so $S_N$ is the orthogonal projection minimizing $\|f - S_N\|$.
Because the coefficients are chosen at random.
questions.explanation: The residual $f - S_N$ is orthogonal to each $e_n$, hence to the whole subspace, so $S_N$ is the orthogonal projection of $f$. By the Pythagorean theorem any other element of the span is farther from $f$, making $S_N$ the unique best $L^2$ approximation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The map $f \mapsto (\langle f, e_n \rangle)_n$ for a complete orthonormal set is an isometric isomorphism between $L^2$ and which sequence space?
questions.explanation: The coefficient map identifies $L^2$ with $\ell^2$ isometrically: Parseval gives $\|f\|_2^2 = \sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2$, and Riesz-Fischer gives surjectivity. Thus every separable Hilbert space is isometrically $\ell^2$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which statement about norm convergence versus pointwise convergence in $L^2$ is correct?
options: $L^2$ convergence implies a subsequence converges almost everywhere, but not necessarily the whole sequence pointwise.
questions.explanation: If $f_n \to f$ in $L^2$, the Riesz-Fischer proof extracts a subsequence converging a.e., yet the full sequence need not converge pointwise anywhere (the moving-bump example). Conversely pointwise convergence need not give $L^2$ convergence without domination.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The trigonometric system $\{e^{inx}/\sqrt{2\pi}\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$ is a complete orthonormal set in $L^2[-\pi, \pi]$. What does completeness give for the classical Fourier series?
options: Every $f \in L^2$ equals its Fourier series in the $L^2$ norm, and Parseval's identity holds.
Every $f \in L^2$ equals its Fourier series pointwise everywhere.
Only continuous functions have Fourier series.
The Fourier coefficients are always zero.
questions.explanation: Completeness of the trigonometric system means each $f \in L^2[-\pi,\pi]$ is the $L^2$-limit of its Fourier partial sums and $\|f\|_2^2 = \sum_n |\hat f(n)|^2$ (Parseval). Pointwise convergence everywhere is a deeper and more delicate matter, not guaranteed by $L^2$ completeness.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Which of the following is a Hilbert space?
questions.explanation: The sequence space $\ell^2$ has the inner product $\langle a, b \rangle = \sum_n a_n \bar b_n$ and is complete, hence a Hilbert space. The spaces $L^1$, $L^\infty$, and $\ell^1$ are Banach but their norms violate the parallelogram law, so they are not Hilbert spaces.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: If $\sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2 < \|f\|^2$ strictly for some $f$, what does this indicate about the orthonormal set $\{e_n\}$?
options: It is not complete; some part of $f$ lies outside its closed span.
questions.explanation: Strict inequality in Bessel means $f$ has a nonzero component orthogonal to the closed span of $\{e_n\}$, so the set fails to be a complete orthonormal basis. Completeness is exactly the case where Bessel becomes the Parseval equality for every $f$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Compute $\|f\|^2$ for $f = 3e_1 - 4e_2$ where $\{e_1, e_2\}$ is orthonormal.
questions.explanation: By the Pythagorean identity for orthonormal vectors, $\|3e_1 - 4e_2\|^2 = |3|^2 + |{-4}|^2 = 9 + 16 = 25$. (The norm itself is $5$, but the squared norm asked for is $25$.)
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The completeness of $L^p$ (Riesz-Fischer) is typically proved by showing that a Cauchy sequence has which feature?
options: It has a rapidly convergent subsequence whose telescoping series converges absolutely, pinning down an a.e. limit that lies in $L^p$.
It is bounded, hence convergent by Bolzano-Weierstrass.
questions.explanation: The standard proof picks a subsequence with $\|f_{n_{k+1}} - f_{n_k}\| \le 2^{-k}$, so $\sum_k |f_{n_{k+1}} - f_{n_k}|$ converges a.e. by the monotone convergence and triangle inequalities. This identifies an a.e. pointwise limit in $L^p$, to which the original Cauchy sequence converges in norm.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Names and statements in Hilbert space theory.
questions.solution: The theorem stating that every $L^p$ space is complete is the {{1}} theorem. The inequality $\sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2 \le \|f\|^2$, valid for any orthonormal set, is called {{2}}. When this becomes an equality for every $f$, the orthonormal set is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Completeness of $L^p$ is the Riesz-Fischer theorem. The coefficient bound is Bessel's inequality, and the orthonormal set is complete (a basis) exactly when Bessel becomes Parseval's equality for all $f$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Pythagorean computation with an orthonormal set $\{e_1, e_2, e_3\}$. Let $f = 2e_1 + 6e_2 + 3e_3$.
questions.solution: Because the $e_k$ are orthonormal, the squared norm is the sum of squares of the coefficients: $\|f\|^2 = 2^2 + 6^2 + 3^2$, which equals {{1}}. Taking the square root, the norm $\|f\|$ equals {{2}}. The Fourier coefficient $\langle f, e_2 \rangle$ equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Orthonormality gives $\|f\|^2 = 4 + 36 + 9 = 49$, so $\|f\| = 7$. The coefficient of $e_2$ in the expansion is read off directly: $\langle f, e_2 \rangle = 6$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: The coefficient map and its target space.
questions.solution: For a complete orthonormal set, sending $f$ to its sequence of Fourier coefficients gives an isometry from $L^2$ onto the sequence space {{1}}. The identity $\|f\|^2 = \sum_n |\langle f, e_n \rangle|^2$ that makes it an isometry is called {{2}}. Surjectivity of this map, that every square-summable sequence is some $f$'s coefficients, is the content of the {{3}} theorem.
questions.explanation: The coefficient map is an isometric isomorphism $L^2 \to \ell^2$. The norm-preserving identity is Parseval's identity, and the surjectivity (realizing any square-summable sequence) is the Riesz-Fischer theorem.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)

questions.stem: Banach versus Hilbert among the $L^p$ spaces.
questions.solution: Every $L^p$ space with $1 \le p \le \infty$ is a Banach space because it is {{1}}, by the Riesz-Fischer theorem. Among them, the only one whose norm arises from an inner product is $L^p$ with $p = $ {{2}}. For that space the parallelogram law {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Riesz-Fischer makes every $L^p$ complete, hence Banach. Only $p = 2$ gives an inner-product norm, and for $L^2$ the parallelogram law holds, which is the structural signature of a Hilbert space.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions II course (MATH 462)
